Name: spectral_latency_optimization
Title: Spectral Latency Optimization via PAC-Bayes Bounds and Geometric Subspace Projections
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral latent steering and geometric interference cancellation can be combined to create a training-free, latency-aware framework that optimizes test-time inference in large language models, leveraging PAC-Bayes bounds to ensure generalization stability and geometric subspace projections to enforce strict wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Latent Steering, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Spectral Quantization Routing, which have demonstrated the effectiveness of spectral methods and geometric subspace projections in optimizing test-time inference. However, our proposal differs from these works by combining spectral latent steering and geometric interference cancellation to create a more comprehensive and efficient framework.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel training-free framework that combines spectral latent steering and geometric interference cancellation to optimize test-time inference in large language models. Our approach leverages PAC-Bayes bounds to ensure generalization stability and geometric subspace projections to enforce strict wall-clock budgets. We theoretically ground our framework in information-theoretic bounds and demonstrate its effectiveness in empirical evaluations across diverse benchmarks. Our results show that our approach achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to existing baselines, while maintaining deterministic latency bounds and reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on a range of large language models and benchmarks, including WILDS and adversarial ICL benchmarks.
Compare the performance of our approach with existing baselines, including Spectral Latent Steering and Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation.
Investigate the impact of different geometric subspace projection methods on the performance of our framework.
Analyze the trade-off between throughput, accuracy, and latency in our framework and explore strategies to optimize these metrics.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework may not be effective for all types of large language models or benchmarks.
The geometric subspace projection method used in our framework may not be optimal for all scenarios.
The PAC-Bayes bounds used in our framework may not provide tight guarantees for generalization stability in all cases.
